feed.title: Combined summary - Subject: Eltoo / LN-Symmetry on Inquisition Signet: BIP 118 + 3-Round State Chain (APO+CTV)

entry.title: Combined summary - Subject: Eltoo / LN-Symmetry on Inquisition Signet: BIP 118 + 3-Round State Chain (APO+CTV)
entry.summary: Aaron Zhang recently conducted a sequence of experiments on the Bitcoin Inquisition 29.2 signet to explore state chain dynamics using various Bitcoin Improvement Proposals (BIPs). He successfully implemented a three-round Eltoo-style state chain, leveraging APO (defined in BIP 118) for state updates and CTV for the final settlement process. This setup involved six transactions, all of which were confirmed on-chain. The transactions utilized a Taproot tree structure comprising three distinct leaves designed to support template hash checks, signature verifications, and sequence verifications. Each state update effectively advanced the state while ensuring that only the latest state could lead to a valid settlement, embodying the core principle of the Eltoo mechanism. In a notable demonstration of flexibility within this framework, Zhang also shared details of another experiment involving a CSFS+CTV ladder variant. This approach replicated the three rounds of state updates characteristic of the Eltoo model but reduced the transaction count from six to two. Instead of relying on APO or CAT, this variant used already-active CTV combined with CSFS, based on Jeremy Rubin's CSFS rekey ladder construction. This method simplified the state progression to mere signature delegations rather than full transactions, thereby collapsing the entire update sequence into a singular settlement witness, showcasing an innovative compression of the state chain process. Furthermore, Sergio Demian Lerner discussed enhancements to the ln-symmetry branch of CLN, incorporating various improvements and addressing questions related to alternative opcode sets like CSFS+TH+IK. These discussions highlighted ongoing explorations into opcode functionalities and their potential implications for network operations without requiring additional soft forks. For example, Sergio pointed out the potential of OTS-based Payment Channels as an alternative to achieve similar functionalities to those provided by proposed soft forks, as detailed in a recent publication available [here](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.04021). These discussions and experiments underscore the dynamic nature of Bitcoin's protocol development, where developers continuously test and refine new methods to enhance scalability, efficiency, and security. Links to the detailed discussions and source code for these experiments are available at Delving Bitcoin for the [Eltoo state chain construction](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/eltoo-state-chain-on-signet-three-rounds-six-transactions-apo-ctv/2413), and the [CSFS+CTV ladder variant](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/eltoo-state-chain-on-signet-again-three-rounds-two-transactions-csfs-ctv-with-rekey-and-ladder-no-cat/2430), along with Aaron Zhang's [source code repository](https://github.com/aaron-recompile/btcaaron/tree/main/examples/eltoo).
